content.full_text:
State of California
Fair Political Practices Commission
P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 • • • 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814

May 17, 1976

Sherman C. Wilke
Law Offices
Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray
Downtown Plaza Towers
555 Capitol Mall, Suite 1100
Sacramento, CA 95814

Re: Opinion Request
    No. 76-061

Dear Mr. Wilke:

     Thank you for your letter of May 3, 1976, asking whether the
law firm of Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray is an employer of a
lobbyist within the meaning of the Political Reform Act. Because
your letter raises no substantial question involving an inter-
pretation of the Political Reform Act, no formal opinion will be
issued. However, I hope the following informal comments will be
helpful.

     Based on the facts stated in your letter, the law firm of
Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray is not the employer of a lobbyist.
See 2 Cal. Adm. Code section 18619(b). However, the assets of the
firm may not be used to make gifts aggregating more than ten dol-
lars in a calendar month to any state candidate, elected state
officer, legislative officer or agency official, or to make camp-
aign contributions to a state candidate, a committee supporting a
state candidate, or an elected state officer. 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18619(f). In addition, if Mr. Fleury uses the facilities
of the law firm to support his lobbying activities, he should
report the appropriate portion of the office rent, office util-
ities, compensation of employees, and other office expenses as
overhead expenses incurred by the lobbyist. 2 Cal. Adm. Code
Section 18615.

     If you wish to appeal the denial of your opinion request, you
may do so pursuant to 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18321. If you have
any additional questions, please contact Natalie West, an attorney
with the Legal Division of the Commission staff.

                                        Sincerely,

                                        [signature]

                                        Michael Bennett
                                        Executive Director

MB:plh

LAW OFFICES
WILKE, FLEURY, HOFFELT & GRAY
DOWNTOWN PLAZA TOWERS
SUITE 1100
555 CAPITOL MALL
SACRAMENTO, CALIFORNIA 95814

SHERMAN C. WILKE
GORDON A. FLEURY
RICHARD H. HOFFELT
JOE G GRAY
WILLIAM A. GOULD, JR.
PHILIP H. BIRNEY
THOMAS G. REDMON
ROBERT J. MCEWAN
DONALD REX HECKMAN II
ALAN G. PERKINS

PAUL PECK
OF COUNSEL

TELEPHONE
(916) 441-2430

May 3, 1976

Fair Political Practices Commission
1100 K Street
Sacramento, California 95814

Attention: Mr. Kenneth Finney, Chief of
Legal Division

Re: Request for Opinion

Dear Mr. Finney:

This opinion request is based upon the following
recitals:

1. It is made pursuant to the provisions of
Section 18320 of Division 6 of Title 2 of the Administrative Code.

2. It is made by Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray,
a law partnership, whose duties, if any, under the Political
Reform Act of 1974, are in question.

3. It is joined by Gordon A. Fleury, who is a
partner in said law firm and a registered lobbyist.

The question upon which your opinion is requested
is as follows:

IS THE LAW FIRM OF WILKE, FLEURY, HOFFELT
& GRAY, A PARTNERSHIP, AN "EMPLOYER OF A
LOBBYIST" WITHIN THE MEANING OF SECTION
18619(b) OF TITLE 2 OF THE ADMINISTRATIVE
CODE, OR OF ANY OTHER PROVISION THEREOF,
AND IS SAID FIRM REQUIRED TO FILE REPORTS
WITH THE COMMISSION AS AN EMPLOYER OF A
LOBBYIST?

The above question and request for an opinion is
based upon the factual situation stated immediately below.

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 3, 1976
Page Two


The law firm of Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray
is composed of six partners and five associates and is
engaged in the general practice of law in Sacramento,
California. One of the partners of this firm is Gordon A.
Fleury, and he has been employed personally and indivi-
dually to render services as a lobbyist for three different
organizations. Mr. Fleury has registered personally and
individually as a lobbyist for each of said organizations,
and each of said organizations, in turn, has registered as
an employer of a lobbyist and in each instance has shown
that its authorized and registered lobbyist is Gordon A.
Fleury, personally and individually. In each instance
above mentioned the employment contract for the lobbying
services is with Mr. Fleury individually and not with the
firm of Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt & Gray. Mr. Fleury has had
a long and distinguished career as a California legislator
(Assemblyman from Sacramento County), a Judge of the
Superior Court (Sacramento County), and a legislative
advocate. His talents, knowledge, experience and skills
are peculiar to him alone, and it was those personal traits
of Mr. Fleury and not the reputation of the law firm that
persuaded the employers to engage Mr. Fleury personally and
individually as their lobbyist. It is certain that in the
event Mr. Fleury should withdraw from his lobbying activities
his client-employers would not retain his law firm as their
lobbyist. They most certainly would look elsewhere for a
replacement lobbyist.

None of the partners or associates of the said
law firm, except Mr. Fleury, has any contact with the said
employers with respect to lobbying activities, and none of
said partners or associates engages in any lobbying activities
on behalf of said employers. Neither the firm nor any of
the partners or associates thereof give any instructions or
guidance to Mr. Fleury with respect to his lobbying activities
on behalf of said employers. All such instructions and
guidance is transmitted by the said employers directly to
Mr. Fleury personally.

Mr. Fleury files regular reports as a lobbyist
and each of said three employers files regular reports as

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 3, 1976
Page Three

employers of Mr. Fleury. The firm of Wilke, Fleury, Hoffelt
& Gray does not file reports as an employer of a lobbyist.

In the case of each of said employers, Mr.
Fleury has instructed the employer to pay the agreed
compensation for his lobbying services by check, payable
to Gordon A. Fleury, individually, and, excepting only
upon one or two occasions (resulting from mistake or inadvertence), the compensation checks have always been payable
to Gordon A. Fleury and not to his law firm.

Mr. Fleury is a general partner in the law firm,
and as such general partner he contributes to the common
funds of the partnership all income generated by him in the
performance of legal services and in the performance of
lobbying activities under the employment contracts discussed
above. All other partners contribute to the common funds
of the partnership all income generated by each of them in
the performance of legal services. All of the costs and
expenses of the operation of the law business of said firm
are paid from the common partnership funds, and each
partner shares in the profits of the firm in accordance
with his general partnership interest in the firm. The
law firm does not make political contributions as a firm.

When the Regulations were adopted by the Commission
the law firm studied the same and concluded that it was not
an employer of a lobbyist and was not required to file any
reports as an employer of a lobbyist. It was, and is, the opinion of the firm
that it does not come within the definition of an "employer
of lobbyist" as said term is defined in Section 18619. The
firm concluded, in good faith, that the spirit and purpose
of the Political Reform Act of 1974 and its Regulations
would be complied with (1) if Mr. Fleury filed the required
reports as a lobbyist, (2) if Mr. Fleury's employers filed
the required reports as employers of a lobbyist, and (3) if

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 3, 1976
Page Four


the firm and its partners complied with all of the
prohibitions against gifts, payments, exchanges and
contributions. Members of said firm have heretofore
discussed with other similarly constituted law firms
the problem which is the subject matter of this letter,
and it is the general opinion of those other firms that
the law firm itself is not the employer of a lobbyist
and is not, therefore, required to file reports as a
firm.

It is the feeling of the undersigned that
the purpose and philosophy of the Act has been fully
satisfied when the registered lobbyist himself, and
the actual employer of the lobbyist (the person paying
the compensation for the lobbying services) both file
the required reports. It would be useless and redundant
for the law firm to file a further report as a fictitious
employer of a lobbyist, for the firm would essentially
be reporting the very same lobbying information already
reported by the actual employers of Mr. Fleury. There
could be no useful purpose served by such additional
reporting.

It also appears to the undersigned that a literal
reading of Section 18619(b), in the light of the factual
discussion stated above, compels the conclusion that the
law firm in question is not an employer of a lobbyist.
The actual employers in question did not retain "A firm,
corporation or other business entity" for the purpose of
influencing legislative action. They retained Mr. Fleury
personally and individually, and they made their compensa-
tion contracts with Mr. Fleury alone and not with his law
firm. It should be immaterial what disposition Mr. Fleury
makes of his compensation after it is received by him, so
long as he does not make any prohibited expenditures. It
should not make any difference whether he expends the
compensation to purchase a new automobile, or to purchase
a new house or to contribute the same to the income of
his law partnership. Furthermore, the Act itself does

78 081

Fair Political Practices Commission
May 3, 1976
Page Five

not contain any provision that would indicate any intent
upon the part of the legislature to require reporting
from law firms under the circumstances outlined above.
The reporting requirements of the Act are contained in
Chapter 6 (Sections 86000 et seq., Government Code) which
regulates the activities of lobbyists. Section 86107 of
the Government Code provides for reporting by lobbyists,
and Section 86108 of said Code provides for reporting by
persons other than lobbyists. The latter section requires
reporting "by any person who employs or contracts for the
services of one or more lobbyists". It is axiomatic that
the Regulations adopted by the Commission must be inter-
preted in a manner as not to be inconsistent with the Act.
If one adheres to the ordinary rules of interpretation
and construction of statutes he is forced to the conclusion
that the law firm mentioned above is not an "employer" of
Mr. Fleury within the meaning and purposes of the Act;
and it would follow, therefore, that the Regulations should
not be interpreted or construed to require the filing of
reports by any class of persons not clearly required under
the Act to file reports. Thus Section 18619(b) of the
Regulations should not be interpreted or construed to
require the subject law firm to file reports as a
fictitious employer of Mr. Fleury.

Your early response to the foregoing request for
opinion will be appreciated.

Very truly yours,

SHERMAN C. WILKE

SCW:kt
